claims.text: Regulation is to blame for the explosion in nuclear construction costs.
Every time nuclear power comes up, the actual construction cost data gets ignored.
French nuclear reactors built in the 1970s cost around 1,000 dollars per kilowatt in today's money.
The two nuclear reactors completed in Georgia in 2023-2024 cost roughly 15,000 dollars per kilowatt.
South Korea built nuclear reactors through the 2010s at about 2,500 dollars per kilowatt.
South Korea has nuclear regulation comparably strict to that of the United States.
The cost gap between 1970s French reactors and the 2023-2024 Georgia reactors is not a regulatory premium.
The real variable explaining differences in nuclear reactor construction costs is continuous build programs.
South Korea built the same nuclear reactor design over and over with the same crews and the same supply chain.
The United States built no new nuclear reactors for thirty years.
After thirty years of building no reactors, the United States attempted a first-of-a-kind nuclear reactor design.
The US workforce that built the recent nuclear reactors had never poured nuclear-grade concrete.
Something like 60-70% of the US nuclear cost gap is attributable to lost workforce experience and supply-chain atrophy, not to safety rules.
The pro-nuclear crowd's favorite fix for high nuclear costs is slashing the regulator.
Slashing the nuclear regulator would recover at best maybe a third of the US nuclear cost gap.
Every construction pause that the anti-nuclear crowd wins makes the next nuclear reactor more expensive.
The anti-nuclear crowd cites the rising cost of nuclear reactors as proof that nuclear power is uneconomic.
The only exit from the nuclear cost spiral is to pick one reactor design and commit to a ten-unit build program.
The first two units of a committed ten-unit nuclear build program will be expensive.
A country that cannot commit to ten nuclear units should probably not build any nuclear reactors at all.

theses.text: The explosion in nuclear construction costs is driven mainly by the loss of continuous build programs -- lost workforce experience and supply-chain atrophy -- not by regulation.
The only way out of the nuclear cost spiral is to pick a single reactor design and commit to a ten-unit build program, accepting that the first two units will be expensive.
Slashing the nuclear regulator would recover at best about a third of the US nuclear cost gap, so deregulation alone cannot fix nuclear economics.
A country that cannot commit to a ten-unit nuclear build program should probably not build any reactors at all.
